title: Smash Ultimate: Art of Pikachu
transcript:
Today's video is brought to you by NAU Drinks.
I'm really happy the guys over at NAU reached out to me as I'm trying to cut down on energy drinks.
NAU Drinks a focus drink designed for gamers. It boosts your concentration and makes you more alert
but without any of the harmful ingredients that other gaming drinks have like caffeine and taurine
which makes it a really great alternative to energy drinks.
I actually really like this drink and I'm starting to favor it over coffee or other energy drinks at this point
mostly because it makes me less jittery and there are a variety of tastes to keep it fresh.
So by clicking on the link in the description you can buy one big bag and get one for free
as well as you can get a 10% discount on everything else with the code is on.
Pikachu has always been one of the better fighters throughout the years.
He's always been mobile, quick, small-sized, and hard to hit.
He has an amazing recovery, a very flexible disadvantage state,
a crazy combo game, and a frustrating ledge trapping game.
His neutral can be played in two ways: either defensive and heavily bait and punish based,
or hyper-aggressive and rushdown based, overwhelming the opponent with quick attacks and multi-hit mix-ups.
His biggest strength is his ability to edgeguard in a multitude of ways
and the amount of options that he can cover while being able to go extremely deep.
It's not only a strength but also somewhat of a necessity in a lot of cases at high-level
for Pikachu to be successful at edgeguarding,
mostly because Pikachu's one of the lightest characters in the game
and avoiding trades or potential rage comebacks is really important.
His aerials, while potentially overwhelming, are still low-ranged and can suffer against longer disjoints.
They also last for a while, so whiffing can sometimes give a close-range opponent
more than enough time to try punishing.
However, all in all, most the time Pikachu is a low-risk, high-reward character
with a steep learning curve that will greatly reward the trainer that chooses him and constantly trains with him.
Typically in neutral, you'll be focusing on using Thunder Jolt to condition the opponent and controlling the pace of the game.
And, as mentioned before, there are two ways that you can use these tools: defensively and aggressively.
And it's important that you use both as well as you know how and when to transition into the aggressive Thunder Jolt.
The most important thing is understanding how to execute the Thunder jolt correctly
You'll want to full hop and buffer the Thunder Jolt
which can be done easier by pressing a shoulder button to jump and the B button together,
and you'll know that you're doing it right when you can double jump before you've landed.
For defensive purposes, this will allow you to do another immediate double jump Thunder Jolt,
and, for aggressive purposes, we'll get a ton of options that open up for you.
For example, at close range, because you haven't landed yet, you can do an aerial just before you land.
Or, choose to double jump and do an aerial as well for mix-ups.
However, keep in mind that the close-range Thunder Jolts aren't safe, so it's usually best to do it defensively if you're close up.
Or, dash away first, then do a jump B-reversed buffered Thunder Jolt.
The aggressive Thunder Jolt is instead at its best if you're distanced and a bit further away
in which case you'll want to buffer the Thunder Jolt as you jump forward
and then dash as soon as you hit the ground so that you run in together with the jolt,
allowing for a ton of mix-ups and will basically put you at a huge advantage.
If the opponent jumps over the jolt you'll be able to easily anti-air with any aerial
as well as if they shield it, you'll be able to grab them,
and they won't be able to jump or do anything after they have blocked the jolts since they are locked in shield.
The only thing to adjust to is if they start rolling before the block.
If you don't execute the aggressive Thunder Jolt right,
the opponent will be able to block and jump away from the attempted grab.
This is why the right execution is so important.
Of course, if the Thunder Jolt is about to hit, you can start a combo.
And, at higher percents even kill.
Another aggressive and important option is that you can also Quick Attack before you've landed
and instead of double jumping,
in which case you can do a Quick Attack either towards them and diagonally down,
towards them and straight down,
or towards them and diagonally away for safety.
As for the rest of the neutral, he spaces his down tilt
and mixes up the opponents with aerials and Quick Attack.
The shield safety of his aerials are mostly dependent on the matchup
and will be a big part of learning matchups in general.
Understanding how to pressure a shield, as well as which out of shield options to use
as you have so many to choose from.
Most of the time though, you will be aiming to short hop forward air
and either zone yourself back again, or try to cross them up
both as a mix-up or if it's safer against the current matchup.
You could also choose to fast fall it behind them if that's even safer.
Or, you can choose to fast fall it and space it away from them, which is the least safe way
but it can still be good in certain matchups.
Back air is the same and how you can use it, space it, or fast fall it
to get behind or as a mix-up and in front of it.
And, when you land, you become really small,
making you really hard to punish for some matchups as well.
Either way, both the forward air and back air have six hits,
which the opponents should be holding their shield upwards against
to avoid getting shield poked after all the Thunder Jolts or the other stuff that they've already blocked.
The back air, however, when fast fallen will provide a landing hit so you could have seven hits instead
which is really good against people who hold their shield up as it can end up hitting their feet for a shield poke
as well as it may interrupt some out of shield options.
Something cool is that you can manually parry each individual hit from both aerials.
Other neutral mix-ups can be a landed crossed up neutral aerial
which can have maximum of four hits,
a buffered down air that you either zone away with or cross up with
which can also be fast falling for a second hit
As well as you could also do a buffered up air to a second aerial
which isn't safe and can be punished before the second aerial comes out.
As for executing the Quick Attack,
you'll mostly be doing forward on the first one and diagonally down on the second.
It could be used as a long-ranged whiff punish but it's mostly used as a mix-up approach.
And, unless you do it from a bad distance or misspace it,
there won't be too many ways to punish it,
and instead, the opponent will have to try their best in covering certain areas if they predict the Quick Attack.
One thing to keep in mind is that you cannot Quick Attack twice in the same direction.
The angles need to be at least thirty degrees apart.
With a single Quick Attack, you can recover horizontally, vertically, and diagonally,
then you have a vertical recovery,
recovery from below the stage,
horizontal recovery,
and a V-shaped recovery to go below the ledge and prevent yourself from getting hit.
To showcase it, here are all the angles that you can do.
Sometimes, when you are really low straight below the ledge
and you'll need to go upwards with the highest amount of distance in order to do this
you'll need to go for angle 1 then -2, or 2 then −1, and so on.
If you want to recover as far horizontally as possible
you'll need to go for angle 3 to 6, which is pretty easy to pull off.
And finally, if you want to recover as far diagonally as possible,
you'll need to go for the 1 or 2 to 5 angle, which can be extremely hard.
It's one of the best tools in disadvantage as Pikachu can always use a double jump and Quick Attack
to try getting out of trouble and land on stage, or land right on you and hit you to try starting something.
It also allows Pikachu to play around ledge traps very effectively
as he can double jump away and Quick Attack to either land right on top of you and back to the ledge
or into the middle stage and even on a platform if there is one.
All of this becomes even harder to do anything about
if he's throwing out Thunder Jolts before landing or recovering as well
especially since they also wrap around platforms
and so tall characters will still have to respect it from the ground.
On top of this, he can wall jump then double jump and Quick Attack as well.
This makes it very hard to ledge trap Pikachu,
but you could always try to hit him before the Quick Attack comes out,
throw out a big or long-lasting aerial to cover yourself as he tries to pass you,
or full hop and try jumping over him and punish where you think he will be appearing,
all of which are pretty hard to pull off.
And, if he gets launched really high up and looking to stall, he could also use Skull Bash.
Otherwise, it's only used as a secondary recovery once your double jump is gone.
And, if you smash the input for Skull Bash, you'll go further than if you only hold forward and press B.
Pikachu's punish game is quite simple, but it does have its layer of depth to it.
Down tilt is Pikachu's main grounded poking tool in neutral.
It can combo into dash attack or mix-up into dash grab at low percents
and then around mid percents dash attack can start being shielded,
so it's better to start fishing for dash grab at that point.
It'll also set up for tech chases around 80, 90%.
Up tilt at low percents can combo into itself and then sets up for up air combos
which you can finish off with almost any aerial.
And after the combo is over, you can try to juggle the opponent
with more up airs, catch landings with forward smash, or Quick Attack for example,
which, as you've seen, also starts a combo.
Unless you hit the Quick Attack around starting percents in which case if you want to be aggressive
you should prioritize getting a grab as the up tilt can be shielded and punished.
Otherwise, you could shield after or dash away to reset your aggression.
Something else you can do is up tilt into an up air at high percents
and, if you read a double jump, you can double jump yourself in place
with a Thunder to catch their jump and potentially kill.
A landed forward air can combo into a grab,
dash attack,
forward tilt, or down tilt.
But, it's a bit inconsistent as sometimes the opponent will pop out which doesn't allow you to combo
A buffered forward air, however, at low percents and either combo to itself or a reverse up air
which you can then choose to do almost anything with.
But, most reliably, we'll be going for a neutral air to drag the opponent down.
The landing neutral air is Pikachu's best combo starter from the air as it basically combos into up tilt or grab.
At higher percents, it can also combo into up smash or down smash to kill.
Getting it back here at low percents can lead into more back airs
that drag opponents offstage, as well as you can finish it off with up airs.
Then from there, it's mostly about reading whether they will recover low or high
and try covering as many options as possible.
The landing head of back air can also set up for a tech situation at around 40, 50%.
Down throw will be Pikachu's main combo throw as it leads to his basic bread-and-butter combos.
It's important at this point to understand how his up pair works.
If you hit with the starting or middle hitbox you'll basically be launching the opponent upwards
but there is also a late hit almost in front of Pikachu that barely launches the opponent,
allowing you to combo even better.
As well as you could get fancy with a pair to forward air drag downs at specific percents
as well as you can full hop and time your forward air,
fast fall it for a drag down, and get a re-grab.
Something extremely helpful is that just as Pikachu slams the opponent that there will be a blue glow
indicating where the opponent is is DI-ing.
If they DI away from you, it'll tilt away from you and if they DI in, it'll go straight up.
If they don't DI at all, there won't be any glow at all.
This actually happens on all moves on all characters,
but, on Pikachu, it shows up earlier than usual for some reason.
The most busted thing about Pikachu is his neutral air loop combos.
This specific combo will only work from starting percents to roughly 30%
depending on the weight of the character.
Either way, after the down throw you'll want to react to the DI
in which case the blue glow can be really helpful.
Then, you'll always want to short hop and slightly delay the neutral air
and fast fall between the second and third hits so that you don't get the last hit.
Once you get the first neutral air, you'll want to first try to re-grab and then start going for up tilts
except against heavyweights, in which case you want to grab three to four times instead before up tilting.
Then, it's up to you and the matchup whether you want to end the combo upwards with an up tilt or down tilt.
Obviously, you don't only get this loop from a grab.
You can even start it from a forward air,
back air,
or a simple tilt.
At the same time, SDI or platforms may or may not disrupt this combo.
You can, however, up throw and then full hop up air and double jump up air
then start a drag down on the platform of Battlefield.
Most characters won't be able to avoid this combo.
Floaty or tiny characters might be able to SDI upwards
and for the rest of the cast, the best thing you can do is try to be as random as possible with DI
or try to SDI behind Pikachu on the very first loop where he needs a grab.
Finally, we have one of the hardest kill confirms in the game: up throw Thunder,
which requires you to be frame-perfect to get.
To clarify, after the up throw you have to run for exactly 7 frames
and on the 8th frame, you should already turn around and jump with down B.
If you are off by even one frame they'll either an air dodge the thunder
or you won't be hitting the thunder on yourself.
This combo will be extremely easy if they don't DI at all
and, if they DI behind Pikachu, it won't work at all.
If you expect the opponent to DI behind you, you can try to down throw instead
which can also lead into a Thunder.
And, at even higher percents, that will continue to work as long as you jump, double jump, Thunder.
This combo once again is extremely hard to get and requires a ton of practice since it's purely timing-based.
It also doesn't really work on all characters.
Either way, the threat of it will definitely scare and condition the opponent.
But, keep in mind that this kill confirm isn't that necessary
as Pikachu will have a ton of other ways to kill anyway.
Dash attack is mostly used for killing opponents at high percents
but it's very punishable against shield.
Up smash out of shield or during a tech chase can kill as well
as forward smash which has long range and lingers for 15 frames
and, if max spaced, can sometimes be unpunishable for some matchups.
Forward tilt can kill at the ledge or set up for tech situations
as well as 2-frame if you do it downwards.
And, even up throw can kill at really high percents.
Mostly, however, you will be finding kills offstage during the edgeguarding phase.
Edgeguarding will be the biggest thing that you'll have to learn when playing against different matchups
all of which can be edgeguarded in their own way.
It's important to know how far you can go offstage
and how far you can push it with aerials until you have to recover.
The craziest thing is that you can go offstage to try covering low
and at the same time still try to cover high with Thunder.
It can also jump offstage with Thunder Jolt to cover low
and then also try to cover mid-distance with Pikachu or even high recoveries.
He also has a few tricks.
For example, he can fall offstage with back air and the opponent will have to try teching at the right time.
Or, he fast falls at the end of it as a mix-up to have the opponent pop out and air dodge if they tried teching.
You can Skull Bash towards the ledge and pull down to try 2-framing.
You can also try to fall with up air and connect into Thunder for a spike.
And, if you're offstage, and someone is trying to 2-frame you,
then you can Skull Bash to stall and double jump in place with Thunder to hit them.
If you don't get the edgeguard you can always try to go to the other side of the stage
to not risk getting reversed on.
If the edgeguard seems too hard to get or if it's too late you have an amazing ledge trapping game anyway
as you can cover ledge jump really well with forward air at the same time,
as it can be super effective against regular get-ups and rolls, and still be reacted to.
As forward smash can also cover all options except for roll
unless he spaces himself slightly back to actually try covering the roll,
in which case you can safely ledge jump.
He can also jump and Thunder Jolt to force the opponent to press a button at a specific timing
and then go for either of the previous options
as well as shield and go for back air to counter ledge roll,
grab to counter a regular get up an attack,
or full hop up air to catch a ledge jump.

title: Smash Ultimate: Art of Sonic
transcript:
This video is sponsored by Anime Apparel
Store, a brand that delivers high
quality, super comfy clothes with tons
of awesome designs. Right now, you can
grab limited time Isos smash merch or
check out the rest of the collection
over at animeappareltore.com.
Use the code is a smash at checkout for
20% off. Also, subscribe to this
channel, my second channel, Isaw Plus,
and follow me on my social medias to
stay updated on upcoming giveaways in
collaboration with Anime Apparel store.
[Music]
Speed is everything when it comes to
Sonic. While most characters rely on
jumping with landing aerials to pressure
and take control of the game, Sonic
doesn't play that game. He wants you to
chase him so that he can react to where
you're landing and punish you for
missing your moves. His entire game plan
is built around quick movement and
reacting to your impatient mistakes.
Meaning Sonic is a stamina based
character that relies on his mastery of
movement to condition opponents into
playing more preemptively. He's trying
to win the long game. So, fighting
against Sonic is all about your reaction
speed and endurance. A huge part of the
game is about gaining a percentage lead
and holding on to it for as long as
possible. Whoever is ahead
percentage-wise doesn't have to be the
chaser. They just have to try to react.
And for Sonic, that's exactly where he
thrives. If he's in the lead, he's
dictating the game. But if you're in the
lead, he has to start taking risks and
rely a bit more on his mix-up
approaches. Mastering his movement is
what's going to make or break you. If
your movement is clean, he will become
slippery, frustrating, and nearly
impossible to catch. However, being fast
on the ground with Sonic is simple
enough. What's difficult is learning how
to stay fast and mobile while airborne,
as one of Sonic's biggest weaknesses is
how slow and vulnerable he actually is
in the air. unless you learn how to move
with spin dash and all of its tricks.
Another weakness is that he's also a
lightweight who can struggle to kill
unless he's mastered edgeguarding or
through spin charge forward airs.
[Music]
Sonic's burst range is way further than
anyone else's, which makes playing
neutral against him incredibly difficult
and almost completely reliant on
cornering him. Dashing in and out of the
opponent's burst range is super
important to bait opponents into an
approach while also remaining at the
range where you can dash tilt for safe
pressure. And while Sonic is all about
speed, sometimes just walking is great
for positioning yourself better and
changing the pace of the game. Sonic's
landing aerial pressure is generally
terrible. The only exceptions are
neutral air and up air, but even those
are extremely hard to find because of
their low range. Unless you're looking
to punish spot dodges, regular getups,
pressure platforms, attempt to juggle
landings, or as a rare mixup. Either
way, if neutral air crosses up a shield,
it'll be super safe. and landing up air
will be safe no matter what and is
always recommended to be followed up by
a dash away. Other aerials such as
forward air and back air have a much
bigger focus on anti-airing short hops
and full hops. They can work out of
shield in some situations, especially
with still down air being the fastest
option, but Sonic's out of shield game
is generally weak, which is why Sonic
tends to play a bit more defensive.
Spin dash is Sonic's most important
move. Side B and down B are very
similar, but serve different purposes.
SideB, also known as spin dash, is a bit
more defensive as it takes a bit longer
to charge up, is a bit slower at coming
out, and only hits once, so it provides
a bit less mixups against shield.
However, as soon as it's released, he'll
gain six frames of invulnerability
during the initial hop, making it
excellent at getting through projectiles
and punishing approaching aerials. The
max charge won't have an initial hop,
but will still have invulnerability.
SideB can also be jumped while it's
being charged. On the other hand, down
B, also known as spin charge, is a lot
more aggressive as you can quickly mash
down B to charge it up faster. It'll
immediately move forward faster as there
is no initial hop. And it'll hit
multiple times depending on how much you
charged it, making it better for long
ranged whiff punishing, shield pressure,
and mixups. If you charge it up and
release it in the air, it'll beat
certain projectiles as well. Once both
of these are released and rolling,
you'll only be able to jump out of it.
And once you jump, you'll only be able
to double jump, attack, or press a
special move. You can somewhat cancel
side B during the initial hop by double
jumping and air dodging down or down
airing. Down B, however, can easily be
cancelled into a down air at any point.
And by pressing attack or jump, you'll
launch yourself upwards instead. This
does work for side B as well. If you
press attack while charging, keep in
mind that once you've spent your double
jump and spin dash onto the ground,
you'll still be out of a double jump
until you touch the ground with your
actual feet. Even though these moves are
extremely fast, Sonic is still very
vulnerable while spin dashing since the
hitbox is smaller than the hurt box, so
it still loses to disjointed or
longlasting aerials. While usually it'll
just clank with grounded moves, which is
why aerials are more reliable. Be
careful with how you jump against spin
dash though, as not only is spin dash
amazing at punishing landings, but it
can also be jumped with to anti-air.
However, it'll still lose to superior
aerials. One advanced technique is that
after the initial hop, if you time a
jump just as you touch the ground,
you'll short hop the spin jump instead
of full hopping it. So, shielding is
just as important for dealing with it in
neutral. And even though you can't
directly punish spin dash out of shield,
you can still outplay where Sonic ends
up after the spin dash. Once Sonic
commits to spin dash against shield,
it'll be all about mix-ups. The most
common play is to jump right after you
hit a shield to move as far away from
the opponent as possible. And if Sonic
threw out an aerial thinking spin dash
was about to hit, then you'll easily be
able to punish him. And even if he
doesn't jump with an aerial, Sonic will
still be at a disadvantage as he'll be
above the opponent or offstage, which is
the worst possible position for him.
Another mixup includes spin, dash, jump
into differently timed homing attacks,
which if charged can be hit before the
attack even comes out. And even if it
hits shield, it'll typically be unsafe
and punishable with something quick. You
can even react or mash with rolls to
cause it to miss completely, making
Sonic extremely vulnerable for a long
time once he hits the ground. Another
mixup is to spin dash jump into a B,
which either shield pokes you or
interrupts you acting too quickly out of
shield. If it doesn't hit, however, then
Sonic will be in an awful position as
he'll be limited to falling down with
fast fall or no fast neutral air dodges,
awful landing aerials, or a down air.
Sonic can also upbe fast and hold down
just as he lands, letting him skip all
landing lag. If he hits your shield with
the initial hop of side B, he'll also be
able to immediately jump upwards for
potential mix-ups. And if you time the
jump just as you touch the ground,
you'll do a mini jump instead for
another potential mixup. On rare
occasions, he'll just simply mix up by
stopping right next to your shield,
which is extremely risky. Side B will
either end up stopping right in front of
the shield or roll past it with enough
charge only to stop a bit further away,
which still makes him vulnerable. Down
B, however, will more than likely always
end up stopping just behind the shield,
unless you've charged up to max and
immediately change direction back
towards them for another round of
pressure or mixups. Either way, the most
common and safest option after hitting a
shield is to spin jump. This is
important as Sonic might immediately get
cornered while jumping and even end up
off stage and he'll most likely look to
spin dash in from being off stage at the
ledge or while cornered. Any of these
spin dashes from disadvantage should not
be respected with shields. Instead, they
should always be challenged, as this is
one of Sonic's most vulnerable moments,
especially once he spin jumps and then
double jumps for a spin dash back into
the middle, as he'll be locked into a
very specific travel direction with no
way of getting out of the spin dash.
This is why Sonic might prefer to spin
dash from the corners or play with
platforms so that he can mix up his
landings after spin jumping. The better
you are at outplaying his spin dash, the
more he'll be forced to rely on his
other specials and normal moves, and
those aren't nearly as strong as most
other characters. While jumping and
shielding is a good counterplay to spin
dash, once you're at high percents, you
need to be careful with how you jump, as
the easiest way for Sonic to find a kill
is with a full hop back air. If you
avoid that, then he's forced to look for
edge guards or commit to more risky
setups with spin dash or close-range
neutral airs or up airs. Ultimately,
you'll want to condition opponents into
stressfully jumping or shielding against
your spin dashes. Once you've done that,
it's important to start cancelling your
spins and mix up dash grabs, anti-airs,
and punish landings to play a
neverending conditioning game that keeps
the opponent guessing and turns most
situations in your favor. If they aren't
respecting spin dashes at all, just stay
patient, play defensive, and wait for
them to overextend so you can punish
them.
Also, keep in mind that homing attack
can be decent at punishing exceptionally
mashy or jumpy opponents as well, and
will lock onto opponents from Sonic's
burst range, unless Sonic is turned
around, in which case the range becomes
much shorter. Be careful, though, as it
can still miss if the opponent fasts or
moves. If you hit homing attack around
the corner at mid%, you'll be able to
follow it up with a fast fall back air
if they mash attack to go low or double
jump back air if they double jump for a
50/50 setup. In fact, back air comes out
fast enough to immediately punish any
air dodges. This means that you can spin
dash into a reverse neutral B and
attempt this 50/50.
[Music]
[Music]
You'll also be able to spin dash to the
other side and jump out of it to reverse
your double jump aerials.
[Music]
One extremely important advanced
technique for Sonic is spinshot. The
input is simple. Side B to A. However,
it's a frame one input. One way of
explaining it is that the timing of
pressing A after side B is as if you're
strumming a guitar string. Press A too
early and you'll go up. Too late and
you'll waste your double jump. meaning
it's extremely risky if you haven't
mastered the timing. It is an amazing
option though in disadvantage and great
for getting around projectiles. This
also works for spin charge by mashing
down B while airborne, then releasing
the control stick and jumping at the
same frame. Another advanced technique
is spin charge into C stick down. This
is an easy execution and the best way to
fling yourself off stage as quickly as
possible to edgeguard opponents deeper
than usual.
[Music]
When launched off stage, Sonic tends to
spin dash towards the stage, which
allows him to go low or even mix up with
a spin shot. After the low spin dash,
you'll have to double jump and spring
jump, which makes you invulnerable at
frame 5 to 7, meaning he's still very
vulnerable recovering low. After the
spring jump, you will still be able to
air dodge and even wall jump. You'll
also be able to do it higher up than
usual to surprise opponents with an
aerial. If you're sent high off stage
without a double jump, you'll still be
able to homing attack to aid your
recovery. Otherwise, spring jump can
serve as a great way to escape combos or
even as an edgeguard. Keep in mind,
however, that if you hit the spring,
it'll no longer serve as an active
hitbox. The spring can also be planted
mid-stage after a KO to disorient
opponents respawning. You could also try
recovering with a mixup homing attack
above the ledge, especially if you think
you can whiff punish the opponent.
However, if they dodge it, you'll be
going down and need to recover from
really low. So, at least make sure you
don't attempt to homing attack from
below the ledge or you'll end up dying.
If you can find a way to always safely
spin dash into the middle from the
ledge, then it'll always be a good
option. Otherwise, you might just be
forced to regularly get up and run into
the middle. And if you feel limited at
the corner or while off stage, you'll
have to just try your best playing as
patiently as possible.
The rest of Sonic's move set is used for
specific situations and works as a
necessary complement to his overall game
plan. Jab is a fairly safe poking tool,
and the last hit will be somewhat
long-ranged and even push shields,
making it a little harder to grab. On
hit, it can set up for tech chase
situations into spin charge or dash
grab. Forward tilt is solid out of
shield, great at quickly whiff
punishing, and catching landings. If you
space it well, it might be safe in
certain matchups. If you pivot forward
tilt, you'll only hit a single hit to
set up for a tech chase. Down tilt is
also typically safe if spaced and forces
the opponent into a reaction. Use it to
read a mashed attack, air dodge, or a
panic jump. It'll combo if they di and
can be used to force platform tech
chases. Uptilt is used to catch landings
on platforms or extend combos. Up air,
however, will typically be better at
catching landings as it has a great
disjoint and provides a better reward.
Dash attack is a fast burst option for
long ranged whiff punishing and puts the
opponent into a landing situation.
Forward smash has amazing range and is
safe on shield of spaced. It's one of
Sonic's strongest moves to read
opponents with and two framing options
for early kills. Down smash is a weaker
but faster two-framing option and up
smash can be used to catch certain
landings of bigger characters or to
shield poke when the opponent's shield
is low. Forward throw and back throw are
used to launch opponents off stage with
back throw being a potential kill throw
at very high percents. Down throw sets
up for tech chases, but it can be died
down to tech earlier, giving you a
chance to avoid a punish from Sonic. At
higher percent, it'll be used to launch
opponents off stage at a lower angle.
And up throw is a combo throw at low to
mid percents, simply leading into up
air, neutral air, or forward air.
[Music]
The only consistent combo aerials are up
air and neutral air. Forward air might
combo on DI in at low percents, but
against DIOA, it won't lead into
anything. At low percents, up air leads
into multiple aerials, and you'll be
able to extend the combo further using
platforms.
You can finish things with back air and
in some cases follow it up with neutral
air unless the opponent DIS away. At
higher percents, up air will lead into
spring jump aerials for combos and kill
confirms.
And you'll also be able to hit a single
hit of up air into grab or up tilt.
Neutral layer's initial strong hit will
combo into grab tilts
and eventually aerials,
but it's the late weak hit that becomes
more valuable at high percents as it can
combo into almost anything and open up
multiple kill confirms. Spin dash and
spin charge combos are a bit more
advanced. First of all, there are four
launches to be aware of. Hitting spin
dash sends more upwards. Hitting the
initial spin hop has a similar angle but
a shorter launch. Hitting the spin dash
jump sends them horizontally. And
hitting the spin charge sends them more
diagonally. Keep in mind that you can
attempt to SDI down and away against all
of these hits to potentially pop out of
these combo starters, especially spin
charge and even up air combos. Anyways,
all of these launches combo into an
aerial or a homing attack. Spin dash on
its own will mostly only combo into back
air, unless you time your jump exactly
as it hits, in which case you might be
able to follow it up with other aerials.
This makes it decent at low to mid
percents or to combo off of platforms if
you can successfully hit the up air.
However, spin dash is less effective for
killing unless you land it at very high
percents into spring jump, which will
only work if they DI in.
The initial spring hop will usually only
lead into a simple aerial combo for some
extra damage. The spin jump, on the
other hand, can potentially be used as a
kill confirm at higher percent around 80
to 100%. Depending on DI. Spin charge,
however, can be a more consistent combo
starter at low emit percents because of
its multi-hit. Its real strength,
however, is that it'll be the best at
getting kill confirms off stage since it
sends at a perfect angle for a much
larger percentage window. And typically,
you'll only be reacting to two types of
DI. Either they DI away, which you
simply forward air against for a kill,
or they DI in, which requires a double
jump back into forward air. It can also
lead into down air at very specific
percents if they diy down or down and
away.
Sonic's edgeguarding will be one of the
most important ways to secure early
kills. This is one of the reasons why
Sonic should be launching you off stage
a lot more than attempting to send you
upwards. Whether you're edgeguarding
through back airs, forward airs, down
air spikes, springs, homing attacks, run
off stage, and spin dash towards the
ledge, or even forward smash two frames.
Sonic is also able to go really low for
an edgeguard and immediately also go
high because of spring jumps. And homing
attack can be great for edgeguarding
specific recoveries which are slow or
linear. If no edgeguard is found, he'll
shift into ledge trapping, using forward
tilt to cover regular getups and rolls,
and neutral air, which is amazing at
covering regular getups and ledge jumps,
while also allowing him to react to get
up attacks or ledge rolls with a dash
grab. He'll also use retreating short
hop or full hop back airs to catch ledge
jumps and forward smash against
predictable regular get-ups, get up
attacks, or aggressive double jumps in.
Most importantly for Sonic, however, is
to not allow the opponent back into the
middle stage. Focusing on covering
grounded ledge options and then
attempting to catch landings after a
ledge jump is already a strong strategy,
especially with how good spin dash and
spin charge are for punishing landings.
Either way, Sonic will try to wear you
down, tire you out, make you impatient,
and capitalize on your lack of
understanding of where he'll end up
after a spin dash. So stay focused, stay
patient, and learn how to keep hitting
him after spin dashes. Turning his
strongest move into a weakness is how
you break Sonic's endurance and make him
feel limited.
[Music]

title: Smash Ultimate: Art of Young Link
transcript:
What is up everyone! So, recently a lot of people have been asking me if I still do Smash Ultimate sessions
And yes, actually I still do and I never stopped. I've been doing it now for roughly four years
Typically, I do anything from four to seven sessions every day, except Fridays and Saturdays. Anything from 8 A.M. to 8 P.M. works
just fine with me. Any player level is welcome.
I've had tons of sessions with people who literally got Smash one week before their session.
Obviously I've also had a ton of mid-level players,
High-level players and even top-level players that you currently see in tournament! Most of which probably want to remain nameless. Either way,
They all get much better with each session.
There's been a ton of students who couldn't win *a single game* in tournament and now they're either winning
Their locals or consistently making top three!
So the price is currently
$15 USD for a 20-minute session, or
$35 USD
for a one-hour session. And if you want to book one, (or just generally get more information about the session) then you can click the
Discord link below, join it and
private message me. Also, sessions are also streamed to allow you to re-watch your
Session on Twitch. Unless you don't really want it to be streamed, which, is fine, It doesn't have to be. Anyways, during the stream
I mention everything that you do, don't do, could do better (or differently)
And we'll also talk about it right after. As well as I'll teach you anything that YOU need to learn at your current level.
I'll answer any question, and I'll give you a plan for what to work on and how.
Also, usually High to Top level players don't usually fight me for a Best of 5, instead
what they do is that they bring me VODs to
Review and that way, I guess it's more relevant to them because they learn how to beat what they need to beat in the current
Moment. Anyways, feel free to hit me up in Discord and enjoy the video! :)
With each Legend, there has been a worthy hero.
None of them as legendary and fierce as the little fairy boy himself, Link the Hero of Time. Full of energy, courageous,
determined, nimble, and quick on his feet,
He'll keep you out with projectiles,
Fight you up close if he needs to, and if anything ends up hitting, he'll combo the hell out of you.
He's a pretty technical character to play with a very flexible playstyle. His main weaknesses are his recovery,
He can struggle to find a kill and, he has really low range.
Especially if you're at mid-range distance with him, where it would be risky for him to throw a projectile.
His neutral is really hard to explain, as it's really about using every single tool in his kit.
However,
Starting off simple and progressively getting more complex, is generally better than immediately starting off complex and all over the place.
Neutral is less about how you play, and more about how you make the opponent play around all the projectiles and setups. You either throw
Projectiles from a distance and convert off of it if anything is about to hit, as well as punish how they try to work around
The projectiles to approach you. This, in combination with moving in and out with close range pressure is really important as his close range pressure
Can be amazing, because of its safety mix of potential and ability to start some awesome combos.
Let's start off with close-range pressure, and combos. Neutral-Air has an initial sweet spot and a
Long-lasting sour spot. Both of which are safe against shield.
Only the sweet spot being a bit safer. Neutral-Air has a ton of mix-ups.
You could buffer Neutral-Air and double jump out of it with another Neutral-Air, or any other option.
The first Neutral-Air IS punishable
If you're quick enough however. You can short hop and initiate a Neutral-Air low on shield
Which is the safest way to pressure a shield. As you can Neutral-Air right after, jump away with Boomerang,
Shield right after, which is only good if you've spaced the Neutral-Air or, if you crossed it up,
Making it even safer.
You could also full hop Neutral-Air and fastfall right on top of shields.
Which not only beats full hops really well, but it will also stuff out jumps out of shield, and can be generally safe against shield
But definitely not the safest version as some characters can potentially punish it. :(
This is why you could also full hop Neutral-Air, delay the fastfall, and land with another neutral air low on shield.
Which again, is the safest version. At the end of the day however,
The best part of Neutral-Air is its anti-air potential, and
Mix-up potential. Using all the variations of Neutral-Air in a creative way will always be better than only sticking to one. As, as soon
As you become predictable, the Neutral-Air will start getting punished by: Up -Smashes with punishes, and even perfect shields.
In fact, as long as you land Neutral-Air as low as possible on shield
You could jab to try interrupting aggression from the opponent, as long as it's not too fast.
Of course, this can also work from a full hop fastfall Neutral-Air
BUT, it's going to be WAY less safe and reliable, compared to the lowlanded Neutral-Air.
Either way, if you end up hitting a shield with jab,
You could always choose to cancel it after one or two slashes, as it's very safe to do so.
Either that, or you finish it off with a triple jab, or the rapid jab for mix-ups.
Both of these finishers are way more punishable than just ending it with jab one, or two however.
And of course, the best part of Neutral-Air is its combo potential.
Of course, the majority of these combos will get affected by DI.
Which, is the hardest part about his combo game, and that you have to adjust and read the opponent and how you think they will
Escape the combo with projectiles, or other kinds of extensions. At mid percents the sweet spot will start setting up into tech situations,
Which you can choose to punish with a projectile, or dashing against it if you feel like you have the read.
As for the sour spot,
It'll also set up for tech situations at higher percents, in which case you could try angling for the same options.
On top of all of this,
It's one of the best edgeguarding tools because of how long it lasts, and all the things that can lead into.
Neutral could also be played with Forward-Air. And as long as you fast fall and land the second hit as low as possible,
It'll also be safe against shield. Even better,
Most of the time is to land the first hit of Forward-Air, which is also safe.
But, of course EVEN safer if you space it,
Since it'll allow you to Down-Tilt right after. Which also is typically safe against shield, especially when spaced.
Once you land the forward air, you can also jab to intercept opponents, jump Neutral-Air, jump away boomerang,
Full hop high above them,
Shield,
Spot Dodge,
Dash away, or roll away.
Basically, the mix ups are endless and should be adjusted
Most of the time to counter what the opponent typically does out of shield.
He can also: Back-Air into Neutral-Air, Back-Air into Back-Air,
Back-Air into Up-Air or Back-Air into double jump aerial, all of which are really hard to punish.
Hitting the buffered Back-Air will give you A TON of different combos.
The only thing to think about sometimes is that, if you move too much towards an opponent the Back-Air won't fully connect.
So, you'll have to try drifting towards them a bit less for it to fully connect sometimes.
As for hitting the first hit of Back-Air, it will provide you with EVEN more combos.
Especially at higher percents, where you can now start kill confirming with it.
Up-Air is decent against shield as well, if it's landed and crossed up versus a few matchups that struggle to beat it.
Of course the landing hit will always provide a simple combo as well.
Otherwise, it could always be short hopped and buffered to beat full hops or beating people landing on top of you.
But, most of the time it will be used to try killing the opponent.
Speaking of killing, Down-Air it will be perfect for it. Especially the early frames of Down-Air, which is stronger.
Otherwise, the aerial will mostly be used to try landing with, as not only does it have very little landing lag, it needs to be
reacted to very fast to be punished.
But, it can also be auto-cancelled if you know when to land it just as the attack ends. If the aerial isn't fastfallen,
it'll bounce on hits for potential combos,
and on shield, which also is punishable if you react fast enough. Although being a decent mixup in neutral,
It'll mostly be used as a combo finisher. And then he has his Zair. Which shouldn't be something you fish for too much,
But instead occasionally use whenever, you've already
Jumped but Forward-Air will be out of range, or if you feel like projectile will be too committal, then you can land with Hook Shot instead.
And if it hits, it will reward you with a ton of combos as well.
It can also be useful after you jump and throw a bomb.
The Hook Shot will be a really good grab mix-up while grounded, to try with punishing and catching landings. The combos
He gets are pretty straightforward too! From 0 to 20 percentage you'll want to down throw to Back-Air for optimal damage,
and then after roughly 20% you can either down throw or up throw into up air till around 70%. After that it becomes
a bit more of an attempt to try trapping the opponent. As they can only try air dodging or double jumping, both of which you can try to outplay.
Forward throw becomes a kill throw once the opponent are at really high percents,
It's also really fast if you immediately throw. Making it really hard for the opponent to DI correctly.
Back throw is just for throwing opponents off-stage to try attempting an edgeguard.
The Hook Shot COULD potentially be used out of shield,
But it's a bit slow and punishable. Much better is to just Neutral-Air while moving away or go for Spin Attack,
Which will grant you pretty much the same combos as his down throw. From 0 to 20,
You'll want to do Back-Airs or just get an Up-Air.
The only difference is that both you and the opponent have to react to whether the last hit is on the right side, in which
case they need to DI to the right or if it's on the left side, in which case they need to DI to the left, to basically try neglecting the combo.
And again, turn it into more of a trap situation to catch double jumps or air dodges. If they DI wrong however,
It'll lead into Up-Airs for long enough where it MIGHT end up killing you, especially if you jump, double jump Up-Air.
Careful however, as if you are trying to punish something that is TOO safe,
it'll get shielded and punished. Up Smash is another potential out-of-shield option to try killing an opponent or, as a hard commitment to
catch aggressive landings.
And if it hits you'll be hitting the tipper of Up Smash which kills a bit earlier. Down Smash can be used to punish ledge stalls or
As a mixup during tech chasing to launch an opponent off-stage. The Forward Smash is basically only for hard reading an opponent, and punish predictable, or bad decisions.
Forward Tilt is occasionally used as mix-up aggression to try keeping some of the smaller characters out. Up Tilt is a decent anti-air to catch landings,
And if you connect the back hit, you'll always be able to combo at lower percents.
Dash Attack is a potential aggressive mix-up to punish landings and withs.
It can also kill at really high percents. And finally, there's the Deku Shield which will block almost any projectile when standing still,
Crouching,
or walking slowly. And it's way more
Beneficial than shielding or parrying. Be careful though as, projectiles can be aimed to hit your head or feet.
First off, most of the time projectiles should be thrown or picked up while moving.
Standing still while using them, stales mobility greatly. Good projectile play requires you to understand how the opponent should play
against projectiles. The general rules for playing against projectiles is to separate the projectile Avoiding Game, and
Approaching Game. Rather than trying to avoid projectiles and approach at the same time. As that just means that your movement and options were
Manipulated by the projectiles you tried avoiding, and you forced an approach, which is exactly what Young Link wants.
Instead, once again, you should patiently try to avoid and work around projectiles so that you can get into a good position first.
Once you are in a good position,
You can finally start playing the Approaching Game.
The right position will typically be here, where Link's range is too low to
contest you, while also being in a position where it's too risky for Link to throw projectiles.
And, even then, while in this position, not only should you approach,
But you should also mix-up with faking out your approaches, to bait Link into preemptively throwing out an option in anticipation of your approach.
This is also why it's important for you to move while throwing a projectile. As soon as you see that the opponent might be in
A position to approach. These exchanges are what separates the Good Links from the Bad Links.
It's also recommended to not buffer projectiles so much or, in other words, not do so many preemptive
projectiles, and instead try your best timing each projectile to avoid falling into a habit of just
Mindlessly throwing projectiles. And so, projectiles are not just used to actually hit the opponent and deal damage from a distance,
But any projectile that is avoided can be equally as valuable,
as long as you punish the way they are trying to avoid it. Arrows won't really combo at low percents, and will only
realistically combo around mid percents if you jump in while shooting them. And so, if they hit at low percents you either have to read
What they will do after they get hit for a potential followup. Or just keep throwing projectiles.
This is also another benefit to approach at close combat and try and winning neutral, to instead start an optimal opening.
Jumping away and B-Reversing an arrow can be great for starting combos.
But most importantly will be through experience and time, to learn all the crazy arrow combos that exist, and the percents for how to extend into even more arrows, and
Potentially find kills. As knowing when you can short hop in after the first arrow or full hop in, can clutch games out for you.
The Boomerang can also be good at low percents.
Especially when you jump in with it aggressively at close-range, since it's safe against shield.
And it will always provide crazy combos.
The mid-range or long-range Boomerang won't really combo till mid to high percents into an arrow. And if you jump in with the arrow
You'll be able to follow up you into more and even potentially kill.
The Boomerang can be adjusted up or down to cover full hops or slightly up or down to cover short hops.
You can do a soft and a hard throw, depending on whether you tilt or smash the input. As well as you can jump and
B-Reverse the Boomerang for mix-ups. If the boomerang is missed
It'll come back and serve as really good pressure, as if the returning Boomerang hits it'll connect into anything.
As well as you can knock them into the Boomerang with almost any move, and get a flashy combo.
Otherwise it can also come back and break combos, or condition shields and jumps that can be anti-aired.
Bombs should only be picked up when it's safe, and called for. Mostly for additional projectile pressure. Pulling out bombs
when you are being rushed down or stressed in general, is usually a bad idea. Bombs will always combo into something.
However, the explosion is a multi hit so the launch is always a 50/50.
Sometimes they get launched to the left, and sometimes to the right.
So, some things will just have to chance to try getting your kill confirms. Bombs are also great out of shield,
Which you can do automatically by holding shield, and pressing jump and attack, making it your fastest out of shield option which also provides combos.
The fuse for the bombs are roughly 4 seconds so, it can sometimes be
Beneficial to quickly pick out one before recovering as it MIGHT save you. One advanced technique is to pull out a bomb, air dodge upwards,
Then double jump and up special to give yourself a little bit more time for the bomb to explode.
You could also pull out a bomb as you recover grab the ledge and time your get up so that it explodes during your invincibility frames, making it harder for them to deal with you.
You can also release the ledge and immediately clip yourself back towards the stage, then quickly C-Stick upwards to up throw the bomb, tech jump the bomb, and use any
other mix-up to get back to stage. (God that's complicated...)
But, most of the time bombs are mostly beneficial for edgeguarding, and ledge-trapping.
The Z-Dropped bomb can cover ledge stalls, double jumps back in, and ledge attacks.
It won't cover neutral get-ups, ledge-jumps, or ledge-rolls however.
Regular get-ups or ledge-jumps you can try covering with Neutral-Air, which is very hard getting in to smaller opponents. Focus too much on this however,
and the opponent will get away with ledge-rolling against you, which is the most annoying option as you'll mostly have to read it, and
Preemptively cover it, with something like a fade back neutral air, dash back Forward Tilt to jump back Boomerang or, an Up Smash read for example.
Which might mean that you miss out on punishing regular get-ups or ledge-jumps.
But, at least they'll still be cornered, and you'll still have a chance at reading how they want to try and getting back into the
Middle stage. And so, most of the time his ledge-trapping will be a bit more
Pre-emptive than other characters to try covering as many options as you can.
You could try planting a bomb that stand at roll distance,
React irregular get-ups or double jumps with an arrow, and then cover rolls with Down Tilt.
But, it's pretty hard and the opponent can just ledge-jump anyway.
Otherwise, you'll also be able to hit people at the ledge with Forward Tilt, Down Tilt, or Down Smash. All of which could potentially kill.
Thank you guys for watching. If you guys enjoyed this content and want to see more of it, then please consider donating to my Patreon.
As it's realistically the only way I can keep doing these videos as, YouTube doesn't really give me much... 😢
Otherwise, you can also follow me on Twitter or Twitch to see me livestream. Until next time! :)
Make sure to subscribe to IzAw and try to donate to his Patreon! His tutorials are awesome! :D

title: Smash Ultimate: Art of Toon Link
transcript:
Legend has it that there is a hero who known for his
mobility, strength, evasiveness, creativity,
and nimble swordsmanship.
He is known to be amazing at keeping you out,
pressuring you from all ranges,
and because of his Hookshot,
he can grab you as you try approaching him.
It is believed that his projectiles are balanced
in priority and utility,
to set up traps and have his opponents constantly
trying to avoid them or jump over them.
As soon as you would move around his projectiles,
he would start swinging at you in the air
to deny your jumps
or cover landings with more projectiles.
This hero was known as Link,
the “Hero of Winds,”
who utilized his Master Sword
to space you out and kill with its power.
Neutral is less about how you play
and more about how you make the opponent play
around all the projectiles and setups,
to either try throwing projectiles from a distance
and convert off of it if anything is about to hit,
or you punish how they try to work around the projectile
to approach you.
You also use the sword and Hookshot to space them out
and play a bait-and-punish game.
Either way, your objective is to keep the ground
covered and pressured,
limit the opponent’s mobility,
and try forcing as many jumps and approaches as possible.
His dash and run speed is extremely fast for a zoner,
making him really hard to catch or hit.
It also makes him a decent whiff punisher,
being able to mix it up with either
dash attack or Hookshot, especially
if the opponent is busy dodging projectiles.
He can also just dash dance or shield dash
with a Bomb in hand
to find whiff punishes and pressure the ground,
which also can condition full hops.
Whiff punishing isn’t going to be their
main focus, however.
Instead, it’s a bit more rewarding to try your best
in catching landings with either your tilts or Bombs,
anti-airing with your aerials,
and spacing against their shield
with your sword.
And so, first you need to learn how to use
the Master Sword.
The aerials you will be using are
forward air, back air, and neutral air,
all of which can be buffered to safely prressure
an opponent’s shield.
It’s only safe
if you drift away from the opponent,
which can be executed easier if you
dash towards an opponent,
release the Stick to stop,
then dash away from the opponent and press
shoulder button to jump together with the C-Stick.
Another thing you can do is
attack cancelled neutral air.
This is done by dashing towards an opponent,
then releasing the Stick to stop,
do a tilt with the C-Stick and, within 2 frames,
cancel it with a shoulder button jump
and drift yourself away.
Or, an easier way to execute it can be to run
towards an opponent,
release the Control Stick so you stop,
then press jump-neutral air and press yourself back
so that you get the maximum amount of drift.
But even then,
these buffered aerials aren’t too practical
and are usually only done as a mix-up.
It’s even better if you land your aerials
as low as possible against shield.
This is really effective with landing
back air or neutral air,
since even if you don’t space,
you can still spotdodge to avoid grabs
or shield to punish attacks out of shield,
meaning you put yourself in a 50-50 situation.
This 50-50 situation can be avoided, however,
if you land and space at the same time…
...or cross up, since now you can safely shield
without risking getting grabbed
and try counterattacking aerials out of shield,
as well as you can do a landing aerial to dash away and
counterattack their attempted attack out of shield
or do a landing aerial
to an immediate follow-up attack.
Forward air specifically is never safe
unless you space it while landing,
giving you the same three options
to work with after:
shielding,
dashing away,
or doing a follow-up attack.
As for combos, they’re pretty simple.
At starting percents,
landing with either hit of neutral air
has a high chance of tripping
and combos into jab or Spin Attack.
Then after 30%,
it will also start combo’ing into anything else,
like tilts, smashes, dash attack,
grab,
back air,
or Bomb if you Z-drop it first.
Starting ~50–70%,
you’ll start forcing tech situations.
And above that percent,
nothing will really combo,
as you can just DI up
and avoid the tech situation as well.
But, obviously,
it depends on the opponent’s weight and fall speed,
so fast fallers will be forced to tech
for longer percents,
and even against floaty characters,
it’ll be less likely to force them at all.
Landing back air combos into
basically anything as well,
always giving you some kind of a reward.
At mid-percents,
it can combo into itself and into a Spin Attack
if they don’t DI or [if they] DI in.
DI away won’t allow any combos.
Same thing with the landing back air
at high percents:
DI’ing in or having no DI can set up
into a kill confirm,
but DI away, and it won’t set up into anything
except Hookshot.
Knowing from what position you can approach from
and how to space it is super important.
You can also do a landing back air and then
short hop forward and shoot an Arrow,
which is a really strong mix-up as soon as
the opponent is offstage,
as the Arrow combos into forward air
as well as you can punish an air dodge
or punish the double jump above the Arrow.
Landing forward air can combo into dash attack,
Hookshot,
neutral air,
or Bombs.
Otherwise, starting at 15%,
it’ll start forcing a tech situation,
giving you an opportunity to read techs
with Boomerang
or up tilts.
Othewise, these aerials will used
to directly call out full hops.
If they never did full hop,
you can neutral air to cover yourself
or forward air of back air
to cover the ground.
And, if they air dodge as they double jump in,
you’ll frame trap them.
You’re also able to short hop forward air
and then double jump neutral air
to deny jumps out of shield
or just apply anti-air pressure with more forward airs,
which is a big commitment.
You’re last aerial in the neutral will be the Hookshot,
which is equally as safe as the other aerials.
Even then, however,
you’ll always want to space the Hookshot
for extra safety,
and it will also provide you with some combos.
Starting ~80%,
it will start forcing a tech situation.
Up air can also be used to call out full hops
as well as try catching landings with,
and because of its disjoint,
it’ll beat out any attack.
You can short hop up air to catch jumps
out of shield as a mix-up,
which also shield pokes if they have half a shield.
And, against characters
with slow or high-hitting aerials out of shield,
you can also land with it and cross up
for pressure,
as hitting it will provide combos.
Down air is your riskiest aerial and can be used
as a mix-up as you try landing.
The early hit will spike,
and as soon as you hit the aerial,
you can start drifting to the left or right.
And, if you double jump and do it
from really far up,
you can accel yourself to the ground
and auto-cancel it.
The landing hitbox sends opponents up
for a combo,
and even if you are slightly off timing,
you’ll always catch double jumps out
and can fastfall to punish air dodge
very easily.
Hitting the ground pushes close opponents away.
It also combos after a footstool,
as well as it pushes throwable items away.
And, you can also use it as a mix-up
after short hop forward air
to beat out attempted dash grabs or dash attacks.
And, against the majority of the cast,
these aerials are really good offstage,
especially since forward air can lead into
another forward air,
down-thrown Bombs can lead into
forward air,
and Spin Attack in general on its own
is amazing for edgeguarding,
as well as you can down throw the Boomerang
to limit the opponent even further.
Spin Attack is your main out-of-shield option
because of its speed, range, kill power,
and because it lasts for so long
that anyone who tries to
land with a safe aerial and spotdodge
will just get caught.
It also beats anyone who lands with a low aerial
and tries to follow-up attack.
However, if the opponent
lands low with a safe aerial and shields,
it won’t be safe to Spin Attack—
unless you parry first.
It’s also really good at catching
regular get-ups and ledge rolls at the same time.
Down smash can be a hard punish against
ledge stalls
while forward tilt is a safer way of punishing it.
As for your smashes and dash attack,
none of them are really safe against shield.
A spaced forward smash can be really hard
to punish for some characters.
The dash attack isn’t safe against shield,
but to punish it at all,
you need to punish really fast
as it comes out really quick and has little endlag.
And, hitting it will combo into neutral air
unless they DI away
and quickly try double jumping or air dodging,
both of which can still be punishable.
Or, you follow up pressure with forward air
to punish them for not double jumping out.
Either way, smashes are mostly for
hard punishing opponents
or catching rolls or jumps in from the corner.
And, up smash is really important
as an out-of-shield option before a block,
to punish opponents falling with a safe aerial
that normally would not be punishable if blocked.
Up tilt and forward tilt safety
depends on the matchup.
Some characters don’t have a way to deal with them,
making them safe.
Otherwise, if they can punish them,
you’ll need to be a little more careful using them.
The inner hitbox of forward tilt can combo into
dash attack or dash grab.
But, starting ~25%,
it’ll start forcing a tech situation.
There’s also a back hit that can be used
for combos.
And, it’s really good after a landed aerial,
as it covers aerials out of shield,
shield releases,
and rolls.
Hitting the up tilt can consistently lead into
an up air kill confirm.
Down tilt will be safe in all matchups, however,
giving you the option to shield after,
dash away,
or attack again.
The lower the percents,
the bigger chance there is for it to trip.
Either way,
hitting the down tilt allows you to combo into
dash attack or Hookshot.
And after ~60–80%,
it’ll force a tech situation.
And, jab can be used to
get the opponent off of you
and for resetting the opponent.
The only time to mix up
how many jabs you do is if you accidently
started hitting a shield.
As for punishing 2-Frames,
you’ll mainly be using forward tilt.
But, you can also down smash,
down air,
Boomerang downwards,
or Bomb downwards.
Now that you’ve learned how to use the Master Sword,
you’ll also want to make use of the Hero’s Shield—
which can block almost any projectile
when standing still, crouching, or walking slowly—
as it’s way more beneficial than
shielding or parrying.
Be careful, though,
as projectiles can be aimed to it your head.
Mainly in the neutral,
you will be throwing projectiles,
but the Sword is what allows you
to take advantage of these projectiles,
as you will be manipulating the opponent’s
movement and decision-making.
Besides, all your projectiles can be effectively
reflected or hit & denied,
so you can’t just fully rely on them.
All of your projectiles are equally good
for what they do.
Your projectiles can also be thrown
after a simple combo is done,
to cover the air and force out
an evasive option
or cover the ground to force out
a double jump
and have them land somewhere else.
Projectiles are, of course, all good
for edgeguarding
or to throw at the opponent before recovering
to try preventing an edgeguard.
The Boomerang isn’t safe at close range,
but you can jump in a B-reverse it for safety’s sake.
You can do a soft or hard throw
depending on whether you tilt or smash the input.
It can be adjusted up or down
to cover full hop
or slightly up or down
to cover short hop.
Only the close to midrange Boomerang will combo.
You could try DI’ing away the Boomerange
to avoid the follow-up,
but because of his air speed,
you’ll most likely get combo’d anyway.
And, even if you throw it and miss it,
it’ll come back and serve as really good pressure,
as if it hits it will connect into anything,
as well as you can knock them
into the Boomerang
with the Hookshot, jabs, Arrows,
or forward tilt.
Otherwise, it can also come back
and break combos
or condition jumps that can be anti-aired.
Bombs should only be picked up
when it’s safe or called for.
Pulling out Bombs when
you’re being rushed down or stressed in general
is usually a bad idea.
Having one in hand means that
you can start shield dashing a lot more
and play a bit more defensive,
especially since they last for five seconds
and you’ll have a really good out-of-shield option
in all directions that lead into anything,
as well as you can also apply good pressure
from above now.
And, even when you throw it downwards,
the Bomb can lead into forward air or Spin Attack
for a kill.
It’ll also be a really good way of beating full hops
without risking a trade.
You can even jump and Z-drop the Bomb
as a Frame 5 option out of shield
to counter close-range aggression.
This is very easily done by holding shield
while pressing Jump and Attack.
If you throw a Bomb on the opponent’s shield,
it’ll bounce on their shield,
allowing you to recatch it and rethrow it.
The Bomb will blow up after it absorbs 6% damage
or if it connects with fire.
This also allows the Bomb to negate other projectiles
and bounce off of them.
You could pull out a Bomb as you recover,
grab the ledge,
and time your get-up
so that it explodes during your invincibility frames,
giving you a chance to blow up opponents
as well as hide behind the explosion.
Z-dropping the Bomb can, of course, be used
as mix-up pressure
and is also really useful during a ledge trap...
...especially since you can also
buffer a Z-drop after ledge jumping
for a trap.
Arrows can be used from a distance whenever,
as long as you are safe shooting them.
There’s very little lag to shoot one.
They fly very slow.
And, if shot from a distance,
you can run in with one if you want to
and punish a shield,
anti-air a jump,
or try catching a dodge.
They’re really annoying to deal with
and are basically just used to poke the opponent.
You can also short hop and buffer the Arrow,
which gives you one frame to double jump
before landing,
meaning you can also waveland
after an Arrow.
To pull this off consistently,
you’ll need to set the the “A+B to Smash”
and have a shoulder button set to “Jump.”
Then, you press shoulder button together with the A & B,
and you’ll always short hop and buffer the Arrow.
Doing this with a Bomb in hand allows you
to press Grab to drop a Bomb
as a trap, as well.
to rarely and potentially punish a dash grab,
dash attack,
or jump in aerial,
since they might hit the bomb instead.
And, if you short hop
and shoot an Arrow forward from long range,
you can combo into dash attack or Hookshot.
Charging the Bow will only be useful against
someone who refuses to approach,
to damage their shield and make it more likely
for you to shield poke.
And of course,
Arrows can also be used if you want to
B-reverse and mix up your landing.
Once you become fluent at
using all the projectiles and the Sword,
you’ll start to get better at
covering more and more options,
which naturally over time
will make you ledgetrapping better,
as you’ll always have the entire stage to work with.
You don’t have to keep them cornered
but prevent them too hard
from getting up from the ledge,
as they still have to somehow get to you
once they’re up.
The Hookshot should be used occasionally
in the neutral to simply counter shields
but will generally be a risky option.
At low percents, you can forward throw
as you can immediately pressure with dash attack
to punish them trying to tech
or land with an attack,
and if they jump,
you can try reading it as well.
Your down throw is purely a Rock-Paper-Scissor:
you’ll either punish them for trying to attack
with you own attack,
punish their air dodge...
...or punish their double jump
with your own full hop double jump aerial.
Up throw will be your main throw
most of the time,
as it sets up for landing traps, frame traps,
and combos at specific percents.
From ~20–50% if executed just right,
it’ll combo into up air, Spin Attack, or back air.
Even if the up air doesn’t combo,
you should be able to put on a trap,
as if they air dodge,
the up air should last long enough for you
to be able to punish it anyway,
And, if they double jump,
you might hit them out of it.
And, even if they succussfully
double jump out,
you are still able to fastfall
and try covering their landing.
It also starts killing at extremely high percents,
while back throw starts killing a bit earlier,
especially if thrown from the ledge.
The Hero of Winds does struggle against
rushdown close-range combat.
Even though there are ways to handle it,
it can be overwhelming.
You mostly have to rely on blocking
and risking using Spin Attack
or anti-airing them before you block
to get them off.
Or, you simply roll out of the situation,
as your rolls are really good.
Also, he can struggle against anyone
with more range than him.
And, just like the Hero of Time
and the Hero of the Wild,
he has his own strengths and weaknesses
and his own unique playstyle,
which through determination and confidence
can be overwhelming to deal with,
for this Hero wasn’t chosen by the gods
but claimed their power by his own strength.
Hello, everyone.
I just want to quickly let you guys know
that if you want to purchase these,
then you can actually get them in the link
in the description below or in the comment section.
The price can be anything from cheap to expensive,
depending on if you get, you know,
a good quality canvas
or if you just get a poster or whatever.
Also, of course, you can decide yourself
if you want to buy all of them or just some of them.
In any case,
I’m gonna be making a separate video on this
for more more information
and show you guys how it looks
and the quality of it.
Thank you guys for watching!
If you want to support this channel,
then please consider helping me out on Patreon.
There’s a lot of awesome rewards you can get,
you’ll get sessions,
and you’ll also get to watch these “Art of _____’s”
way earlier than anyone else.
And, if you can’t support me on Patreon,
then please consider sharing these videos
to your friends.

title: Smash Ultimate: Art of Chrom
transcript:
what is up everyone so recently a lot of
people have been asking me if I still do
smash ultimate sessions and yes actually
I still do and I never stopped I've been
doing it now for roughly four years
typically I do anything from four to
seven sessions every day except Fridays
and Saturdays anything from 8 a.m. to 8
p.m. works just fine with me
any player level is welcome I've had
tons of sessions with people who
literally got smashed one week before
their session obviously I've also had a
ton of mid-level players high-level
players and even top-level players that
